nativeRange.habitat: Deep lagoon and seaward reefs; forms mid-water aggregations along steep slopes at depths of approximately 1–50 m

diet.notes: In the wild Naso vlamingii is primarily a mid-water zooplankton feeder (FishBase classifies it as such), but in captivity it is most commonly maintained as a leaning-herbivore: dried marine algae sheets (nori), spirulina-based preparations, and macroalgae offered on a veggie clip at least three times per week. Mysis, enriched brine, and other meaty foods are generally accepted alongside tankmates. A vegetable-heavy diet is commonly considered important for tang health, immune function, and HLLE prevention.

summary: A large, active Indo-Pacific surgeonfish with a pronounced rounded snout and electric-blue vertical markings that adults can flash or conceal. Generally considered peaceful toward most reef fish but has been known to be aggressive toward other tangs. Best suited to very large aquariums with long swim corridors and a secure lid.
careNotes:
Naso vlamingii is among the larger surgeonfishes in the aquarium trade and is generally considered to need substantial swimming space rather than a tall footprint. Recommended minimum tank volumes vary widely across sources: Top Shelf Aquatics suggests 200 gallons (~760 L) of an established system, Aquarium Arts/sgreefclub list 250 gallons (~946 L), Cincinnati Coral recommends 240 gallons (~910 L) initially with 300+ gallons (~1136 L) and an 8-foot footprint long-term, and LiveAquaria advises 360 gallons (~1363 L). The figure used here (1500 L) biases toward the larger end of that spread, consistent with the playbook's tank-sizing guidance for active, large-bodied tangs that require ample open swimming room. Tank length is generally considered more important than volume — an 8-foot (240 cm) footprint is widely cited as a practical minimum.

A tight-fitting lid is generally advised. LiveAquaria explicitly notes that this species is prone to jumping and a sealed top is recommended.

The caudal peduncle bears the sharp, permanently erect spines characteristic of Naso tangs ("caudal scalpels"). Handle with care during netting; injuries to handlers and to tankmates have been reported.

Coloration changes substantially with age. Juveniles are lighter dorsally and grade to darker ventrally; adults develop a brown-to-purple body with vivid blue facial and fin highlights and rows of vertical blue marks that can be flashed during display or concealed at rest. Because of this juvenile/adult divergence, juveniles are sometimes sold without obvious resemblance to mature specimens.

Diet classification is a cross-source disagreement worth noting. FishBase characterises this species as a mid-water zooplankton feeder (omnivore leaning planktivore) while aquarium retailers (LiveAquaria, Cincinnati Coral) describe it as a herbivore requiring substantial marine algae. Reconciled here as omnivore with strong vegetable supplementation — both feeding strategies appear to be accepted in captivity, and an algae-rich diet is commonly considered important for color, immune function, and HLLE prevention in tangs.

Temperature data also showed mild disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22–26°C) while FishBase's preferred range peaks around 25.5–29°C with a mean of 27.6°C. The averaged range (23–27°C) used here represents the midpoint.

Reef safety: all surveyed sources (LiveAquaria, Cincinnati Coral, Top Shelf Aquatics, Aquarium Arts) report this species as reef safe and unlikely to harm sessile invertebrates.

Conspecific and intra-genus aggression is well documented: Vlamingii Tangs have been known to be aggressive toward other tangs (particularly other Naso species and conspecifics) while remaining peaceful toward unrelated reef fish. Pairs or small groups are only realistic in very large systems.

Like most surgeonfishes, this species is considered susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or with insufficient marine-algae intake.

Breeding in captivity has not been documented; pair spawning is observed in the wild.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for Naso vlamingii. Wild reproduction involves pair spawning along reef slopes. The species is not considered a candidate for hobby breeding attempts.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Confirms scientific name, family, max size (24 in / 61 cm), care level (Moderate), temperament (Semi-Aggressive, aggressive toward other tangs but peaceful with other fish), reef safe, minimum tank (360 gal / 1363 L), tight-fitting lid recommended, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and herbivore-leaning diet with marine seaweed offered at least 3x weekly.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes / subfamily Nasinae), max length 60 cm TL, max age 45 years, depth range 1–50 m, native distribution (East Africa to Galapagos, north to southern Japan, south to southern GBR/New Caledonia, Tuamotus, Micronesia), deep lagoon and seaward reef habitat forming mid-water aggregations, preferred temperature ~25.5–29°C (mean 27.6°C), and characterizes wild diet as zooplankton feeder (omnivore). IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Cincinnati Coral and Aquarium
additional.notes: Additional tank-size data point: 240 gallons (910 L) recommended initially with 300+ gallons and an 8-foot footprint long-term. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament, reef safe, herbivore-leaning diet (nori, pellets, mysis, macroalgae) with small frequent feedings. Used in tank-size averaging and length minimum (240 cm / 8 ft).

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Lower-end tank-size data point: established 200 gal (~760 L) with 72-inch length minimum. Confirmed reef safe, herbivore diet, peaceful toward most fish but aggressive toward other tangs. Used in tank-size averaging (biased larger per playbook).

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Wild pair spawning observed along reef slopes. Not considered feasible under hobby conditions.
